title: Gravity on the Moon
question: How would the same bouncing ball move on Earth and on the Moon?
objective: Use a bounce comparison to explain how weaker lunar gravity changes motion
summary: The Moon's surface gravity is about one-sixth of Earth's, so a ball with the same launch can rise higher and remain airborne longer there.

steps.title: Make a prediction
steps.prompt: Predict what the same upward push would do to a ball under Earth and Moon gravity.

actions.label: Moon bounce is higher
actions.description: Predict that the ball rises higher and falls more slowly on the Moon.

actions.label: Both bounce the same
actions.description: Predict that gravity makes no difference to the bounce path.

steps.title: Run the demonstration
steps.prompt: Release the paired balls with the same simulated push and watch both arcs.

actions.label: Run Earth vs Moon
actions.description: Start the paired bounce animation beside the astronaut pedestal.

actions.label: Skip the demonstration
actions.description: Move to a conclusion without collecting motion evidence.

steps.title: Record the conclusion
steps.prompt: Choose the conclusion best supported by the two bounce paths.

actions.label: Record weaker lunar gravity
actions.description: Conclude that the Moon's roughly one-sixth gravity allows a higher, slower arc.

actions.label: Record equal gravity
actions.description: Conclude that Earth and Moon pull the ball downward equally.

outcomes.title: Testable prediction
outcomes.message: You predicted a higher, slower bounce where gravity is weaker.
outcomes.explanation: A prediction becomes scientific when an observation can support or challenge it.

outcomes.title: A useful guess to test
outcomes.message: That prediction gives the demonstration something clear to challenge.
outcomes.explanation: Mistaken predictions are useful because comparing them with evidence reveals which variable matters.

outcomes.title: No bounce evidence yet
outcomes.message: A conclusion needs the Earth and Moon motion comparison first.
outcomes.explanation: Running the same demonstration under two gravitational strengths isolates gravity as the changing variable.

outcomes.title: Moon motion explained
outcomes.message: Your conclusion matches the taller, slower lunar bounce.
outcomes.explanation: Surface gravity on the Moon is about one-sixth of Earth's, so the same launch is pulled downward less strongly.

outcomes.title: Compare the arcs again
outcomes.message: The Moon ball stayed aloft longer and rose higher than the Earth ball.
outcomes.explanation: The Moon's gravity is about one-sixth as strong as Earth's at the surface, not equal to it.
